coredata.dc:title: Carbon-polymer dot-based UV absorption and fluorescence performances for heavy metal ion detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Jiemin
Zeng, Danhong
Lin, Yichun
Ye, Weihao
Chen, Congcong
Xu, Zhiqiang
Hu, Guangqi
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: In previous reports, carbon dots (CDs) were customarily used as fluorescent probes to detect heavy metal ions. However, scientists neglected to take advantage of the excellent UV absorption properties of CDs to detect heavy metal ions. Herein, we synthesized nitrogen-containing carbon polymer dots (N-CPDs) for the determination of Co2+ ions in water samples by a one-step hydrothermal method using l-histidine and ethylene imine polymer as raw materials. The N-CPDs were characterized by ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–vis), infrared spectrum (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. They possess superior full-band UV absorption performance and the surface is rich in multifunctional groups such as –COOH, –CN–, –OH, etc. When Co2+ was added to N-CPDs solution, the color of the solution rapidly changed from colorless to yellow–brown, which was visible to the naked eye. The UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs changed, and the fluorescence was instantly quenched, due to the formation of chelate between Co2+ and N-CPDs, and the FRET process occurred. The detection of Co2+ showed good linearity for both fluorescence and UV absorption spectroscopy modes in the range of 0–200 μM, and the limit of detection were 1.0023 μM and 0.75 μM, respectively. These two methods have the advantages of simple operation, remarkable selectivity and small sample size, which can be applied to the field detection of Co2+ in water samples. It is possible to develop the UV absorption properties of CDs to detect the ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal ions
UV absorption
Fluorescence
Co2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Co2+ by fluorescence spectroscopy
Detection of Co2+ by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the procedure for preparing N-CPDs for the detection of Co2+.

(a）TEM images of N-CPDs. (b) particle size distribution of N-CPDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of N-CPDs. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CPDs. (e) the fluorescence spectra of N-CPDs. (f) the full spectrum of N-CPDs. (g) C 1 s spectrum of N-CPDs. (h) O 1 s spectrum of N-CPDs. (i) N 1 s spectrum of N-CPDs.

(a) Relative UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs dissolved in organic solvents at 356 nm. (b) Relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) and relative UV absorption intensity (A/A0) of N-CPDs at different pH values (pH 2–12). (c-d) UV absorption and relative fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs dissolved in 0.2 to 2.0 mol/L NaCl solution. (e-f) UV absorption and fluorescence of N-CPDs from 0 to 20 h, respectively. (The inset: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-CPDs with time).

(a) Fluorescence intensity in the coexistence of N-CPDs, N-CPDs and metal ions, N-CPDs-Co2+ and other metal ions. (b) Fluorescence intensity of Co2+ added to N-CPDs solution with time (0–36 min) (The inset: fluorescence spectra of N-CPDs-Co2+). (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CPDs solution with Co2+ (0–200 μM). (d) Relative fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs solution with Co2+ (0–200 μM). (The inset: linear relationship between Co2+ concentration and N-CPDs).

(a-b) Photographs of N-CPDs solution mixing with metal ions under daylight and UV lamp (365 nm) respectively. (c-d) Photographs of N-CPDs solution mixing with different concentrations of Co2+ (0–120 μM) under daylight and UV lamp (365 nm), respectively.

(a) UV absorption intensity in the coexistence of N-CPDs, N-CPDs and metal ions, N-CPDs-Co2+ and other metal ions. (b) UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs solution was added Co2+ with time (0–36 min). (The inset: UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CPDs-Co2+). (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CPDs solution were added 0–200 μM of Co2+. (d) Relative UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs solution were added 0–200 μM of Co2+. (The inset: linear relationship between Co2+ concentration and N-CPDs).

(a) The potential change of 0,100, 150, 200 μM Co2+ added to N-CPDs solution. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CPDs solution and N-CPDs-Co2+ solution. (c) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of Co2+ and fluorescence spectra of N-CPDs. (d) Fluorescence lifetimes of N-CPDs and N-CPDs-Co2+.
A illustration of the possible mechanism of N-CPDs-Co2+.
Detection of Co2+ in lake water and tap water using fluorescence spectroscopy.
Detection of Co2+ in lake water and tap water using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy.
Determination of Co2+ by other optical probes in recent papers.

head.ce:title: Carbon-polymer dot-based UV absorption and fluorescence performances for heavy metal ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In previous reports, carbon dots (CDs) were customarily used as fluorescent probes to detect heavy metal ions. However, scientists neglected to take advantage of the excellent UV absorption properties of CDs to detect heavy metal ions. Herein, we synthesized nitrogen-containing carbon polymer dots (N-CPDs) for the determination of Co2+ ions in water samples by a one-step hydrothermal method using l-histidine and ethylene imine polymer as raw materials. The N-CPDs were characterized by ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–vis), infrared spectrum (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. They possess superior full-band UV absorption performance and the surface is rich in multifunctional groups such as –COOH, –CN–, –OH, etc. When Co2+ was added to N-CPDs solution, the color of the solution rapidly changed from colorless to yellow–brown, which was visible to the naked eye. The UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs changed, and the fluorescence was instantly quenched, due to the formation of chelate between Co2+ and N-CPDs, and the FRET process occurred. The detection of Co2+ showed good linearity for both fluorescence and UV absorption spectroscopy modes in the range of 0–200 μM, and the limit of detection were 1.0023 μM and 0.75 μM, respectively. These two methods have the advantages of simple operation, remarkable selectivity and small sample size, which can be applied to the field detection of Co2+ in water samples. It is possible to develop the UV absorption properties of CDs to detect the ions.

ce:section.ce:para: As the development of society, people are paying more and more attention to environmental safety issues, especially heavy metal pollution [1–3]. Heavy metals are widespread in nature, but the increasing human activities of mining, smelting, processing, and commercial manufacturing of heavy metals contribute to the pollution of many heavy metals [4,5]. Among them, It is well known that cobalt ion (Co2+) has once again attracted public attention because of the severe threat it poses to the environment and human health [6]. Some studies have shown that that excessive Co2+ can trigger skin erythema and itching of the skin. In severe cases, symptoms of acute chemical gastritis with nausea, vomiting, severe pain in the upper abdomen, followed by vomiting blood and blood in the stool occur [7,8]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop an efficient, rapid and convenient method to detect cobalt ions. The traditional detection methods include atomic absorption spectrometry [9], electrochemical method, [10,11] high-performance liquid chromatography [12], flame atomic absorption spectrometry [13], inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry [14], UV–vis absorption spectrometry [15], and fluorescence spectrometry [2,16]. Among these methods, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometry play irreplaceable roles due to their simple operation, low production cost, no tedious pre-treatment and easy analysis.

Carbon dots (CDs) are optical carbon nanomaterials with a particle size of 10 nm or less [17]. Based on the diversity of carbon nuclei, CDs are mainly classified into five categories: (1) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), (2) graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs), (3) carbon quantum dots (CQDs), (4) carbon nanodots (CNDs), and (5) carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [18,19]. As the rising star of luminescent nanomaterials, CDs have adjustable fluorescence, excellent photostability, easy functionalization, nontoxicity and extraordinary biocompatibility [20–22]. These intrinsic advantages facilitate the application of CDs in analyte detection, bioimaging, cancer therapy, anti-counterfeiting and luminescent devices [23,24]. Moreover, using CDs for ion detection has the advantage of convenience, speed, and high accuracy. For example, Yue et al. [25] reported a novel yellow fluorescent CDs that detects oxidative l-glutathione (GSSG) by a fluorescent switching process. Cai et al. [26] used CQDs to measure iron ion concentration. Zhang et al. [27] grafted CDs onto the surface of PVDF-g-PAA membrane for the fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions. Guo et al. [28] reported a nitrogen-fluorine co-doped carbon dots (NFCD) and double emission as a probe for Ag+ detection. Singh et al. [29] developed highly fluorescent CDs for Fe2+ ion detection using mango leaves. Overall, CDs holds great potential as a fluorescent probe for ion detection. However, in previous reports, most scientists have focused only on the fluorescence enhancement or fluorescence quench of CDs to detect ions, ignoring their optical absorption [30,31]. The optical absorption of CDs can not only be used as an explanation for the fluorescence mechanism, but also can be well used in ion detection.

Herein, green nitrogen-doped carbon polymer dots (N-CPDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using l-histidine and ethylene imine polymer (PEI) as raw materials. N-CPDs possess excellent UV absorption properties and contain multifunctional groups such as –COOH, -CN-, –OH, etc. Co2+ can form chelates with N-CPDs, and the color of the solution rapidly changes from colorless to yellow–brown. It induces the enhancement of UV absorption intensity and fluorescence quench, so that the fluorescence spectrometry (FL) and UV–visible spectrometry (UV–vis) can simultaneously detect Co2+ in water samples, and the detection limits are 1.0023 μM and 0.75 μM, respectively. Overall, it is feasible to use UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy simultaneously to detect Co2+.

All chemical reagents were used directly without any purification. Ethylene imine polymer (PEI, M.W. 70 000, 50 % aqueous solution) and metal salts (Co (NO3)2·6H2O, FeCl2 6H2O, ZnCl2, YN3O9·6H2O, LaN3O9·6H2O, SnCl4, GeCl4) from Aladdin ltd. (China). l-histidine and metal salts (HgCl2, Ni (NO3)2, CdCl2, MnCl2) were purchased from Maclean's ltd. (China).

The Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was recorded on Japan-JEM 2100F and was used to investigate the particle size distribution of CDs. Fluorescence spectra (FL) for exploring luminous conditions with the Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. UV–vis absorption and transmittance spectra test for UV absorption and transmittance at 200–800 nm using Hitachi UV-3900 spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra for characterization of functional groups on a Thermo-Fisher Nicolet IS10 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) for sample surface elemental composition, structure using SA-Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer.

The procedure of preparing N-CPDs was according to previous work [32]. First, 1.29 mmol of l-histidine and 0.0064 mmol of ethylene imine polymer (PEI) were dissolved 10 mL DI water and ultra-sonicated for 30 min. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to 20 mL polytetrafluoroethylene liner and heated at 200 ℃ for 6 h. After the solution was cooled to room temperature to get the yellow solution and removal of large particles using 0.22 μm micropores. Then, the solution was concentrated and added to acetone solution, and the solid particles were removed by centrifugation at 15,000 r/min. The supernatant was dialyzed with 500 Da for 2 h, and after the solid was obtained by spin evaporation and finally configured as a 1 mg/mL solution.

To detect the selectivity of N-CPDs for Co2+, all metal ion solutions (Hg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Y3+, La3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Sn4+, Zn2+, Ni3+, Ge4+, Pb2+ and Bi3+) were configured as 200 μM. 400 μL N-CPDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 100 μL of buffer solution (pH = 6.86) were added 3 mL DI water in a cuvette. Then add 10 μL of different metal ion solution. The fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs-metal mixtures was recorded at 440 nm upon 530 nm excitation. The UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs-metal mixtures was recorded at 200–800 nm.

400 μL N-CPDs solution (1 mg/mL), 100 μL of buffer solution (pH = 6.86), and 10 μL different concentrations of Co2+ (from 0 to 200 μM) solution were added 3 mL DI water in a cuvette. The fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs-Co2+ mixtures was recorded at 440 nm upon 530 nm excitation. The UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs-Co2+ mixtures was recorded at 200–800 nm.

As a candidate for ion detection, the stability of N-CPDs is an important parameter. 100 uL of N-CPDs were dissolved in different organic solvents, including N, N-Dimethylacetamide (DMAC), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), Acetate (HAc), Acetone (CP), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Ethyl alcohol (EA), Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), Tetrahydrofuran (THF), Methyl alcohol (MT), and using water as a reference. From Fig. 3 a, N-CPDs can dissolve well in most organic solvents and keep the UV absorption intensity unchanged. In addition, the pH of the solution interferes with the UV absorption properties and optical properties of N-CPDs. Then, the N-CPDs was dissolved at different pH values to determine the stability. As seen in Fig. 3b, the pH did not significantly affect UV absorption intensity and fluorescence intensity. The relative UV absorption intensity gradually decreases (λ = 356 nm, A/A0) and the relative fluorescence intensity (λex = 440 nm, F/F0) gradually increases with increasing pH 2 to 12. N-CPDs surface is rich in functional groups, such as –COOH, -CO- and -CN- bonds, so they can form hydrogen bonds rapidly under strong acidic environment. In the strong base environment, H+ is neutralized and the charge transfer leads to the disruption of the electron leap in the fluorescence center. The change is favorable to the UV absorption intensity enhancement [42]. Thus, N-CPDs have good stability at pH values between 4 and 10. However, to exclude the effect of pH, a buffer solution with pH = 6.86 was added in all subsequent experiments. Subsequently, the UV absorption and fluorescence effects of N-CPDs in NaCl solutions were monitored. The UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs at 356 nm was unchanged when the concentration of NaCl solution was increased from 0.2 to 2.0 mol/L, and no significant change was observed in FL, indicating the excellent salt tolerance of N-CPDs (Fig. 3c-d). Moreover, it can be found that monitoring the salt tolerance of N-CPDs by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is significantly better than fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV absorption and fluorescence of N-CPDs solution have basically remained the same when it was continuously irradiated under daylight for 20 h (Fig. 3e-f). All the above indicates that N-CPDs provides good stability which is applicable in ion detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ by fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: As fluorescent sensors, N-CPDs are important for practical applications to explore their selectivity and sensitivity to Co2+. First, the selectivity of N-CPD for Co2+ was evaluated by fluorescence spectroscopy. Different metal ions were added to the same concentration of N-CPDs solution, including Hg2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Y3+, La3+, Mn2+, Sn4+, Zn2+, Ni3+, Ge4+, Pb2+ and Bi3+, and the fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs was monitored with a maximum excitation wavelength of 440 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 a, Pure N-CPDs solution exhibits the superior optical performance. When the Co2+ was added, the N-CPDs solution instantly dropped. However, the fluorescence was basically unchanged when other metal ions were added to N-CPDs solution. Subsequently, other metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Y3+, La3+, Mn2+, Sn4+, Zn2+, Ni3+, Ge4+, Pb2+ and Bi3+, were added to the N-CPDs-Co2+ solution, respectively. In the presence of Co2+, the fluorescence of N-CPDs remained quenching after the addition of other ions and the burst state was essentially the same. Thus, most of the ions can coexist in the detection of Co2+ without affecting the detection of Co2+. Fig. 4b demonstrates the fluorescence intensity of Co2+ addition to N-CPDs solution with time (0–36 min). The fluorescence of N-CPDs was effectively burst when Co2+ was added to the N-CPDs solution, and this burst occurred quickly. Moreover, the burst state of N-CPDs-Co2+ solution remained the same after 36 min, and the burst stability was well established. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs and Co2+ concentration is shown in Fig. 4c. With the increase of Co2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N-CPDs at around 530 nm gradually decreased. As shown in Fig. 4d, a good linear relationship with Co2+ concentration in the range of 0–200 μM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.995. The linear equation is F/F0 = -0.0153c + 0.868. F is the fluorescence spectra intensity of N-CPDs in the presence of Co2+. F0 is the fluorescence spectra intensity of N-CPDs without Co2+ solution. c represents the concentration of Co2+. The limit of detection (LOD = 3σ/S) can reach 1.0023 μM. Fig. 5 a shows the state of the N-CPDs solution after adding of the 13 metal ions under daylight. Only after the addition of Co2+, the N-CPDs solution has a colorless change to dark yellow. Under the UV lamp (365 nm), the N-CPDs solution after the addition of Co2+ no longer emits green light, while the N-CPDs solution with the coexistence of other ions still emits bright green light (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c-d, the solution can be seen by the naked eye to change from colorless to brown in visible light and from bright green to dark green in 365 nm light with adding of a small amount of Co2+. Therefore, the combination of colorimetric method, and fluorescence spectrometry can achieve selective and sensitive detection of Co2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is a kind of absorption spectroscopy, which itself has many characteristics, such as high measurement sensitivity, high accuracy, wide application range, and the ability to determine many metallic and non-metallic elements and their compounds. However, over the years, the application of CDs for the detection of metal ions has often focused only on fluorescence quench or fluorescence enhancement phenomena. The use of UV–vis spectroscopy as a mechanism to determine the fluorescence quench or fluorescence enhancement, which ignores the fact that it can be used to detect metal ions. Interestingly, the accuracy of heavy metal ion detection using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is comparable or even better than that of fluorescence spectroscopy. Similarly, to determine that N-CPDs can be selective and sensitive to Co2+ by UV–vis absorption spectrum, we evaluated the interference of other heavy metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6 a, 400 μL of N-CPDs with 3 mL DI water was added to a centrifuge tube, and finally different metal ions including Hg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Y3+, La3+, Mn2+, Sn4+, Zn2+, Ni3+, Ge4+, Pb2+ and Bi3+ were added to the solution. Meanwhile, UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded between 200 and 800 nm. The absorption intensity at 356 nm of the prepared N-CPDs was enhanced by the addition of Co2+. However, the absorption intensity at 356 nm of the prepared N-CPDs was enhanced by the addition of Co2+. In N-CPDs-Co2+ solution, the UV absorption intensity did not change significantly when different metal ions were added, indicating that N-CPDs have good selectivity for Co2+ and are not influenced by other ions. Compared with the fluorescence spectroscopy for the detection of Co2+, the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy gave the best selective performance. Fig. 6b demonstrates the UV absorption intensity of Co2+ with time after adding N-CPDs solution. When Co2+ is added, the UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs solution at 356 nm increases instantly and does not change again with time (0–36 min). To achieve the maximum response of Co2+ to N-CPDs solution, some tests were carried out to optimize the sensing parameters. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, 10 μL, 0–200 μM of Co2+ solution was added to N-CPDs solution, and read the UV absorption intensity at λ = 356 nm in UV–vis absorption spectrum. A showed good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99 and the LOD is 0.75 μM. The linear equation is A/A0 = 0.01688c + 4.447. A is the UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs in the presence of Co2+, A0 is the UV absorption intensity of N-CPDs without Co2+ solution. c represents the concentration of Co2+.

In UV–vis absorption spectrum, N-CPDs add the d-d* transitions of the complexes formed by metal ions and carbon polymer dots to the existing π-π*, n-π* transitions. Thus, it is promoting the enhancement of absorption. Co2+ can be regarded as the electron donor and N-CPDs as the electron acceptor, and they combine to form a stable chelate [43]. On the one hand, Co2+ can replace the hydrogen ions or hydrogen atoms in N-CPD to form covalent bonds. On the other hand, Co2+ connects with lone pairs of electrons (O, N) in N-CPD to form ligand bonds. Not only expands the covalent system in the molecule, but also changes the electron-absorbing or electron-giving nature of the original group. As a result, a red-shift phenomenon appears in UV–vis absorption spectrum with the addition of Co2+, and the potential and solution color also change [44]. When 100 μM of Co2+ solution was added onto N-CPDs solution, the color deepens instantly and its potential changed from − 1.85 mV to 1.97 mV (Fig. 7 a). With the increase of Co2+ concentration, the color of N-CPDs solution gradually deepened and the potential gradually increased, which can also indicate the formation of chelate. And the color change caused by the chelate generated by the reaction of Co2+ with N-CPDs depends mainly on the nature of Co2+ and N-CPDs, which is electrostatic in nature. Then the metal ions affect UV absorption intensity in a ligand-like protonation manner, so that a red-shift in the absorption spectrum appears with the addition of Co2+, but the change in the shape of the absorption spectrum is not significant (Fig. 7b).

During the formation of chelates between Co2+ and N-CPDs, Co2+ also causes fluorescence burst of N-CPDs, revealing that N-CPDs can be used as fluorescent probes to detect Co2+ based on them. Six sensing mechanisms are currently proposed as fluorescent probes. They are photoelectron transfer (PET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filtering effect (IFE), aggregation burst (ACQ) and aggregation emission (AIE), respectively. [45,46] As can be seen from Fig. 7c, there is a spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the normalized Co2+ and the excitation spectrum of the N-CPDs, and the addition of Co2+ enhances the absorption spectrum of the N-CPDs. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CPDs and N-CPDs-Co2+ were characterized separately, and their lifetimes decreased from 2.34 ns to 1.79 ns in Fig. 7d. The process is typical of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The FRET system contains two fluorophores, one is the energy donor and the other is the energy acceptor. When the fluorescent probe is excited with the excitation wavelength of the donor, a nonradiative energy transfer occurs from the donor to the acceptor. Therefore, the acceptor emits fluorescence. Fig. 8 illustrates the possible mechanism of N-CPDs-Co2+.

In general, the selectivity and universality of the choice of UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is higher than that of fluorescence spectroscopy by data analysis, because the use of UV–vis absorption spectrophotometry requires only that the probe contains chromogenic groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, ester, nitro and aromatic systems, which will yield UV and visible light absorption. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is obtained based on the determination of the absorption of substances at different wavelengths of light (monochromatic light). It is more selective than other testing methods fluorescence spectroscopy requires a substance to produce fluorescence. The type of substance is discriminated based on the fluorescence spectroscopy (provided that the quantum yield meets the requirement), so it is more selective. But the application has some limitations because not all substances have fluorescent performance.

Finally, we explored the practical application of N-CPDs and the recovery rate of N-CPDs. Tap water and water from Hongze Lake of South China Agricultural University were taken as different water samples. Taking standard Co2+ addition experiments to verify the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Tables 1 and 2
. The recovery of Co2+ using fluorescence spectroscopy ranged from 101.55 % to 99.20 %, and the recovery of the other method ranged from 100.72 % to 99.04 %, indicating that the use of fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is suitable for the detection of Co2+ in the environment. As shown in Table 3 , we analyzed the published papers on the detection of Co2+ in recent years for comparison. This work allows the detection of Co2+ not only using fluorescence spectroscopy but also using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and is comparable to the reported analytical methods.

In summary, a Co2+ sensor with good selectivity and sensitivity was successfully prepared by using a one-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon polymer dots (N-CPDs) with l-histidine and ethylene imine polymer, and by using the property of Co2+-induced N-CPDs to form chelates. During the detection process, the concentration of Co2+ can be detected by simply mixing N-CPDs-Co2+ without any complicated modification process. It has noticeable color change from colorless to yellow–brown, which is visible to the naked eye. It is an economical, simple, convenient and fast way to detect Co2+ concentration by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The use of N-CPDs has broad application prospects in detecting of Co2+ ions and other heavy metal ions.

Jiemin Qiu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Danhong Zeng: Methodology, Data curation. Yichun Lin: Methodology, Data curation. Weihao Ye: Data curation. Congcong Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhiqiang Xu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Guangqi Hu: Funding acquisition. Yingliang Liu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12174119 and 52172142). And the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2019A1515110921).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient luminescent metal-organic framework for the simultaneous detection and removal of heavy metals from water
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.D. Rudd, H. Wang, E.M. Fuentes-Fernandez, S.J. Teat, F. Chen, G. Hall, Y.J. Chabal, J. Li, Highly Efficient Luminescent Metal-Organic Framework for the Simultaneous Detection and Removal of Heavy Metals from Water, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 8(44) (2016) 30294-30303. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.6b10890

TrAC, Trends Anal. Chem.

TrAC, Trends Anal. Chem.

TrAC, Trends Anal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanostar electrodes for heavy metal detection
